title: Causal evidence of a line attractor encoding an affective state

data_scope.animal_model: Male Esr1cre/+ C57BL/6N mice, Swiss Webster demonstrators, BALB/c intruders; all bred at Caltech
data_scope.n_neurons_per_dimension: 45 per dimension (x1 and x2) for n=9 mice

core_conclusion: Using two-photon calcium imaging and holographic optogenetic perturbations in head-fixed mice observing aggression, this study provides the first causal evidence that a continuous line attractor is intrinsically implemented in the mammalian hypothalamus (VMHvl). On-manifold stimulation of attractor-contributing neurons produces progressive integration and persistent activity; off-manifold perturbations are rapidly corrected back to the attractor. Individual differences in line-attractor stability correlate strongly with functional connectivity strength among x1 neurons (R2=0.87), and mechanistic modelling implicates slow neuromodulatory neurotransmission (tau_s ~20 s, consistent with GPCR/neuropeptide signalling) rather than fast glutamate in sustaining the affective internal state.
why_it_matters: First causal proof of a continuous attractor in a mammalian circuit; bridges single-neuron connectivity and manifold-level dynamical systems perspectives; links circuit architecture to individual differences in aggression; implicates slow neuromodulation in persistent emotional states; establishes an all-optical closed-loop paradigm reusable across mammalian social-behavior circuits.
why_nature: Resolves a long-standing open question about continuous attractors in mammalian circuits; dual novelty in experimental approach and conceptual finding; broad relevance to computational neuroscience, psychiatry, and neurotechnology; clean quantitative mechanistic story with precise statistical validation.
added_value_to_my_research: rSLDS real-time latent-structure extraction blueprint -> unsupervised spatiotemporal pattern discovery in urban mobility data
Attractor stability ~ functional connectivity (R2=0.87) -> mechanistic analogy for spatial autocorrelation decay in public health hotspots
Eigendecomposition of connectivity matrix -> network time constant derivation applicable to spatial interaction and road-network graphs
Conservation of on-manifold position -> dynamical invariant discovery for mobility trajectory analysis via optimal transport
Criticality and individual differences -> phase-transition detection methods for urban spatial network tipping points

figures.caption: Attractor dynamics in head-fixed mice observing aggression: rSLDS identifies x1 (integration) and x2 dimensions; flow fields, line-attractor score, and autocorrelation half-widths validated across n=9 mice

Holographic perturbations reveal integration dynamics in the VMHvl: x1 stimulation produces on-manifold integration; x2 stimulation produces transient off-manifold excursions followed by relaxation (n=8 and 7 mice)
Neural implementation of a line attractor by functional connectivity: single-cell stimulation reveals selective x1-to-x1 but not x1-to-x2 connectivity, demonstrating ensemble structure

Mechanistic modelling suggests slow neurotransmission and feedback inhibition: only slow (tau_s ~20 s) neuromodulatory networks with 36% subnetwork density and fast feedback inhibition recapitulate observed integration time constants

The strength of functional connectivity reflects line-attractor stability: individual differences in attractor time constant correlate strongly with x1 functional connectivity strength (R2=0.87, n=8 mice)
